pipeline_models._rationale: Cross-model critique. The drafter (Claude Sonnet) and the critic (GPT-5.5) are different models from different vendors. When they agree something is wrong, the signal is much stronger than one model self-critiquing. The decider (Claude Sonnet again) synthesizes the two perspectives. Same pattern gstack uses for /codex review of Claude-authored work.

series_anchor.label: Charles Comte — Traité de la propriété (Volumes I-II)
series_anchor.palette: stone grey, ink umber, weathered iron, parchment cream, single accent of muted teal
series_anchor.mood: Boundary stones, ironwork, parchment ledgers. The slow inscription of property lines onto land.
series_anchor.watercolor_treatment: Dry-brush stone textures; iron and metal rendered with single concentrated washes that pool to near-black at the deepest point and dilute to grey at the edges; teal allowed only as a small note.

work_metadata.title: Traité de la propriété: VOL II
work_metadata.author: Charles Comte
work_metadata.description: Traité de la propriété (1834) by Charles Comte presents a sweeping philosophical and legal theory of private property. Comte argues that liberty is the foundation of rights, and that occupation and appropriation of things flow naturally from human freedom. He criticizes ancient jurisprudence grounded in slavery, claiming it undermines genuine property rights. He traces the formation of property from individual acquisition of things, through communal and national levels, exploring territory, value and legal guarantees. Comte thus frames property as essential for human development and social progress.

1_context.content_excerpt:
## CHAP. 27: On the creation and distribution of movable properties.

HAVING explained how properties that consist in lands or buildings are formed, and how the territory of a nation is distributed, it will be easy for me to make understood how properties that consist in movable objects are formed: it will be seen that they all derive from the same principle, and that they are created by analogous processes.

The principal object of the appropriation of a piece of land is to draw from it the things that are necessary for the satisfaction of our needs; it is to employ the soil as an instrument

output.reasoning: Volume II shifts from fixed land to movable property — things drawn from the earth and set into circulation. A single iron weighing pan, worn and slightly tilted, evokes the act of measuring and distributing movable goods while staying true to the series' boundary-stone-and-ironwork mood.
output.subject: A single iron weighing pan, tilted slightly off-level, resting alone
output.palette: weathered iron, stone grey, parchment cream with one small note of muted teal
output.composition: The pan sits low and left of center, occupying roughly a third of the warm cream field. It tilts gently rightward, its visual weight pulling the eye down and leaving the upper two-thirds of the paper as open, breathing negative space. No horizon line, no surface indicated — the object floats in parchment emptiness.
output.watercolor_treatment: Iron body rendered with a single concentrated wash of Payne's grey pooling near-black at the deepest interior curve and bleeding outward to a dilute stone grey at the rim — one pass, no reworking. Dry-brush scuffs along the outer edge of the pan suggest hammered metal texture, cold-press paper grain showing through the thin grey. A faint pencil under-drawing is barely legible beneath the wash. One small teal accent, heavily diluted, settles as a watermark at the base where the pan meets nothing.
output.prompt: A single iron weighing pan, tilted slightly off-level, painted in watercolor on warm cream cotton paper. The pan sits low and left of center, its body rendered in one concentrated wash of Payne's grey that pools near-black at the deepest interior curve and thins to a dilute stone grey at the rim — one layer, never reworked. Dry-brush scuffs along the outer edge suggest hammered metal, cold-press paper grain visible where the pigment grows thin. A faint pencil under-drawing ghosts beneath the wash. One small note of heavily diluted muted teal settles at the base. The upper two-thirds of the field is open parchment cream, uninterrupted negative space. No surface, no shadow, no background — only the object and the breathing paper around it.

output.reasoning: Only material issue is weak hedging language that leaves the tilt under-specified; no gate-failing watercolor-label, AI-slop, palette, or subject-alignment failures appear.

The critic's single issue is a medium-severity weak language flag on 'tilted slightly off-level.' Replacing it with 'tilted five degrees off-level' is a concrete, measurable improvement that removes vagueness without bloating the prompt or conflicting with the series anchor. Accept it.

decisions.reason: Specifying 'five degrees' removes the vague hedge and gives the renderer a concrete, unambiguous tilt angle.

output.final_prompt: A single iron weighing pan, tilted five degrees off-level, painted in watercolor on warm cream cotton paper. The pan sits low and left of center, its body rendered in one concentrated wash of Payne's grey that pools near-black at the deepest interior curve and thins to a dilute stone grey at the rim — one layer, never reworked. Dry-brush scuffs along the outer edge suggest hammered metal, cold-press paper grain visible where the pigment grows thin. A faint pencil under-drawing ghosts beneath the wash. One small note of heavily diluted muted teal settles at the base. The upper two-thirds of the field is open parchment cream, uninterrupted negative space. No surface, no shadow, no background — only the object and the breathing paper around it.
output.changes_summary: Replaced 'tilted slightly off-level' with 'tilted five degrees off-level' to eliminate vague hedging and provide a concrete, renderer-actionable tilt specification. No other changes were made.

final_prompt: A single iron weighing pan, tilted five degrees off-level, painted in watercolor on warm cream cotton paper. The pan sits low and left of center, its body rendered in one concentrated wash of Payne's grey that pools near-black at the deepest interior curve and thins to a dilute stone grey at the rim — one layer, never reworked. Dry-brush scuffs along the outer edge suggest hammered metal, cold-press paper grain visible where the pigment grows thin. A faint pencil under-drawing ghosts beneath the wash. One small note of heavily diluted muted teal settles at the base. The upper two-thirds of the field is open parchment cream, uninterrupted negative space.
